abstract: Medical image segmentation serves as a fundamental pillar of clinical image processing. Consequently, the ability to accurately compare results and evaluate segmentation quality is vital for driving progress within the field. Despite its importance, researchers face several persistent hurdles: the complex process of metric selection, the prevalence of inconsistent mathematical definitions for standard metrics in existing literature, and computational inefficiencies that hinder the processing of large datasets. Furthermore, many current evaluation frameworks lack the necessary support for fuzzy segmentation. This paper addresses these challenges by exploring the limitations of contemporary validation techniques and proposing strategies to improve the reliability and scalability of segmentation assessment.
